task_description:
Screen each abstract for relevance to machine learning for genomics and multi-omics data integration in biomedical research. Rate methodological quality.

Relevance: RELEVANT if the paper applies ML to genomic, transcriptomic, proteomic, epigenomic, or multi-omics data to answer a biomedical question. NOT_RELEVANT if the analysis uses non-omics data, if ML is applied to a non-biomedical problem, or if the paper is purely a clinical or imaging study without omics data.

Quality rubric:
  1 = No methodology or purely conceptual.
  2 = Approach mentioned; metrics absent or too vague to evaluate.
  3 = Metrics and dataset described; single or no baseline.
  4 = Rigorous evaluation, multiple baselines, external datasets, and code/data release.

papers.title: Integrating Multi-Omics with GNN for Cancer Driver Gene Discovery
papers.abstract: Identifying cancer driver genes requires integrating heterogeneous biological evidence. We construct multi-omics graphs incorporating somatic mutations, copy number alterations, methylation, and RNA expression from 9,000 TCGA samples across 33 cancer types and train a heterogeneous GNN. The model identifies known driver genes with precision 0.81 and recall 0.76 at a 5% FDR threshold. Comparison against MOGONET, IntegrATE, and random forest baselines is provided.
papers.topic_hint: multi-omics, GNN, cancer driver genes, TCGA, heterogeneous graph
papers.methodology_hint: heterogeneous GNN, TCGA 9k samples, precision 0.81, recall 0.76, 3 baselines, FDR control
papers.claimed_contribution: Heterogeneous GNN for cancer driver gene discovery from multi-omics, precision 0.81, 3 baselines.

papers.title: Machine Learning Shows Promise for Genomic Medicine — A Perspective
papers.abstract: ML and genomics are natural partners. High-throughput sequencing generates data volumes that only ML can fully exploit. This perspective discusses future directions for ML in GWAS interpretation, polygenic risk scoring, and functional variant annotation. No new model or empirical analysis is presented.
papers.topic_hint: perspective, ML, genomics, GWAS, polygenic risk
papers.methodology_hint: perspective, no model, no data, no metrics
papers.claimed_contribution: Perspective on ML opportunities in genomic medicine.

papers.title: Variational Autoencoder for Latent Representation of Single-Cell Transcriptomics
papers.abstract: We train a scVI-based variational autoencoder on 2.3 million single-cell RNA-seq profiles from 14 tissue types to learn batch-corrected cell-type embeddings. Downstream cell-type clustering achieves adjusted Rand index 0.89 versus expert annotations. The representations transfer to novel tissues not seen during training. Code is released with comprehensive tutorials and reproducibility scripts.
papers.topic_hint: VAE, scVI, single-cell RNA-seq, batch correction, cell-type clustering
papers.methodology_hint: scVI VAE, 2.3M cells, ARI 0.89, transfer to novel tissues, public code and tutorials
papers.claimed_contribution: scVI VAE for scRNA-seq batch correction and clustering, ARI 0.89, code released.

papers.title: Clinical Trial Enrolment Prediction Using Hospital Administrative Data
papers.abstract: Identifying eligible patients for clinical trials is labour-intensive. We train a logistic regression model on structured administrative EHR data to predict trial eligibility from diagnosis codes and demographics. Precision 0.71 at recall 0.80 is achieved for a specific oncology trial. No genomic or omics data is used.
papers.topic_hint: logistic regression, clinical trial enrolment, EHR, administrative data
papers.methodology_hint: logistic regression, EHR codes, precision/recall — administrative data not omics
papers.claimed_contribution: LR for trial eligibility prediction from EHR codes, precision 0.71, no omics data.

papers.title: Sparse Autoencoder for Proteomics Biomarker Discovery in Early Parkinson's Disease
papers.abstract: We apply a sparse autoencoder to plasma proteomic profiles from 480 early Parkinson's patients and 480 controls in the PPMI cohort. The latent bottleneck identifies 31 proteins associated with synaptic vesicle trafficking and mitochondrial function as candidate biomarkers. A logistic regression trained on the sparse features achieves AUC 0.82. No multi-baseline or external cohort comparison is included.
papers.topic_hint: sparse autoencoder, proteomics, Parkinson's disease, PPMI, biomarker discovery
papers.methodology_hint: sparse autoencoder, PPMI proteomics, AUC 0.82, LR on features — no external validation
papers.claimed_contribution: Sparse autoencoder for PD proteomics biomarker discovery, AUC 0.82, single baseline.

papers.title: Deep Learning for Lung Nodule Detection in Low-Dose CT
papers.abstract: We train a 3D convolutional network on the LUNA16 dataset to detect pulmonary nodules in low-dose CT scans. Sensitivity 0.96 at 1 false positive per scan is achieved. The model involves CT imaging and disease detection; no genomic, transcriptomic, or multi-omics data is used.
papers.topic_hint: CNN, lung nodule detection, LUNA16, low-dose CT
papers.methodology_hint: 3D CNN, LUNA16, sensitivity 0.96, CT imaging — no omics data
papers.claimed_contribution: 3D CNN for lung nodule detection, sensitivity 0.96, LUNA16 benchmark, no omics.

papers.title: Attention Mechanism for Functional Genomics: Predicting Enhancer Activity from DNA Sequence
papers.abstract: Predicting enhancer activity from DNA sequence alone is a key genomics challenge. We train a dilated convolutional attention network on ENCODE DNase-seq and ChIP-seq data from 218 cell types to predict regulatory activity. Pearson r=0.81 averaged across cell types is achieved. We compare against Basset, DeepBind, and a gradient boosting baseline using k-mer features.
papers.topic_hint: attention network, functional genomics, enhancer prediction, ENCODE, DNA sequence
papers.methodology_hint: dilated convolutional attention, ENCODE 218 cell types, Pearson 0.81, Basset/DeepBind/GB baselines
papers.claimed_contribution: Attention network for enhancer activity prediction, Pearson 0.81, 3 baselines on ENCODE data.

papers.title: Transformer for Predicting Chromatin Accessibility from DNA Sequence
papers.abstract: Enformer-style transformers can capture long-range regulatory interactions. We train a large transformer on 200 kb genomic windows to predict ATAC-seq peak signals across 131 cell types. Pearson r=0.88 on held-out chromosomes is achieved. Ablation confirms that long-range context beyond 10 kb contributes substantially. Code and pre-trained weights are publicly released.
papers.topic_hint: transformer, ATAC-seq, chromatin accessibility, DNA sequence, Enformer
papers.methodology_hint: large transformer, 131 cell types, Pearson 0.88, ablation of context length, public code
papers.claimed_contribution: Transformer for chromatin accessibility from DNA, Pearson 0.88, long-range ablation, code released.

papers.title: Predictive Coding for Clinical Deterioration Using Wearable Physiological Data
papers.abstract: Early warning for clinical deterioration in hospital wards requires continuous monitoring. We train a predictive coding network on continuous SpO2, HR, and respiratory rate waveforms from 28,000 ward patients. AUROC 0.87 for deterioration events is achieved. No genomic or omics data is used; the signals are physiological waveforms from wearable devices.
papers.topic_hint: predictive coding, wearable, clinical deterioration, physiological monitoring
papers.methodology_hint: predictive coding, wearable waveforms, AUROC 0.87 — physiological not omics
papers.claimed_contribution: Predictive coding for ward deterioration detection AUROC 0.87 from wearables, no omics.

papers.title: Graph Variational Autoencoder for Multi-Omics Integration and Disease Subtyping
papers.abstract: Integrating genomic, epigenomic, and transcriptomic data enables richer disease subtyping. We develop a graph VAE that encodes sample-level multi-omics similarity graphs and predicts cancer subtypes. Applied to TCGA breast and kidney cancer cohorts, the model identifies clinically distinct subtypes with significantly different survival outcomes (log-rank p<0.001). Comparisons against iCluster+, MOFA, and SNF are provided alongside code release.
papers.topic_hint: graph VAE, multi-omics, disease subtyping, TCGA, iCluster
papers.methodology_hint: graph VAE, TCGA breast/kidney, log-rank survival, iCluster/MOFA/SNF baselines, code released
papers.claimed_contribution: Graph VAE for multi-omics disease subtyping, survival p<0.001, 3 integration baselines, code released.
